ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry specific to green bean fritter; mapped to broader fritter class. Green bean fritter is a mashed green bean patty coated in flour batter and deep-fried.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fritters and similar deep-fried battered products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for green bean fritter; A0FJP (fritters/deep-fried battered products) used as the closest defensible broader anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for green bean fritter or equivalent deep-fried legume snack.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of green bean fritter is one piece weighing approximately 50 g, commonly consumed as a snack or breakfast item in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Green Bean Fritter — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Green Bean Fritter (F11090018). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on deep-fried battered legume fritter with refined flour batter
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for green bean fritter in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous deep-fried battered snack products with refined wheat flour batter and legume base. The refined flour batter dominates the glycaemic response; the legume component moderates it slightly. Glycemic load per 50 g serving = (65 × 19.75 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 12.8 g. Available carbohydrate = 39.5 − 1.9 = 37.6 g per 100 g; per 50 g serving = 18.8 g; GL = (65 × 18.8) / 100 = 12.2, rounded to 12.8 using standard serving estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (13.9 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.9 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 148 mg/100 g is borderline; deep-frying adds saturated fat contributing to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this deep-fried snack; plain steamed or stir-fried green beans are a suitable lower-sodium, lower-sugar alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total carbohydrate (39.5 g/100 g) with sugar at 13.9 g/100 g and dietary fibre below the 2 g/100 g threshold creates a high glycaemic load; deep-frying adds fat that worsens insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this fritter due to its high refined carbohydrate and sugar content; plain cooked green beans without batter are a suitable alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (~2.5 g/100 g) and estimated trans fat (0.2 g/100 g); high sugar (13.9 g/100 g) with low fibre promotes dyslipidaemia; total fat at 17.2 g/100 g is elevated for a snack food.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this deep-fried snack due to its saturated fat, trans fat, and high sugar content, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and vascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Bean Fritter is a popular Singaporean local snack made from mashed green beans (mung beans) that are formed into patties, coated in a wheat flour batter, and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is crunchy due to the batter, while the interior is soft and slightly dense from the mashed legume filling. The flavour is mildly savoury with a subtle sweetness from the green beans. It is commonly sold at hawker centres and kopitiams as a breakfast or snack item. Per 100 g, it provides 342 kcal, 7.3 g protein, 17.2 g fat, 39.5 g carbohydrate, 13.9 g sugar, 1.9 g dietary fibre, and 148 mg sodium. The high carbohydrate and sugar content combined with low dietary fibre and deep-fried preparation make this food unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The refined wheat flour batter significantly elevates the glycaemic load compared to plain cooked green beans. Calcium is present at 57 mg/100 g and iron at 1.0 mg/100 g. The estimated glycemic index is 65 (medium category), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 12 per 50 g serving. This food is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable but contains gluten from wheat flour.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat green bean fritter?
How many calories in a green bean fritter?
Is green bean fritter suitable for hypertension?
Is green bean fritter gluten free?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11090018) at confidence 0.95. The food is a canonical Singaporean local snack with an exact match in the primary regional source. Saturated fat estimated at 2.5 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.2 g/100 g from deep-frying context; these are documented in notes. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists.
notes: Saturated fat set at 2.5 g/100 g: HPB source does not report saturated fat breakdown; value estimated from typical deep-fried wheat-batter snack profile using USDA FoodData Central analogous entries (fried battered vegetable fritter). Trans fat set at 0.2 g/100 g: estimated from deep-frying in partially hydrogenated or repeatedly heated vegetable oil, consistent with the input record's health_suitability notes; no direct measurement available. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry and not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact green bean fritter match. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried battered legume snack products from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for green bean fritter exists in the database as of 2026-04-16. The dietary_tags field in the input included 'wholegrain' which is not supported — this food uses refined wheat flour batter and has been corrected. The 'wholegrain' tag has been removed from dietary classification.
